answer:
**Iqbal's fable-poems "Ek Makra aur Makhi" (ch. 20) and "Ek Pahar aur Gilehri" (ch. 21) of *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* share a common ethical structure: a dialogue between a physically dominant or imposing figure (spider/mountain) and a smaller, seemingly vulnerable one (fly/squirrel), where the former deploys arrogance or cunning disguised as hospitality/boasting to assert superiority, only for the latter to expose the peril of superficial judgments through wise retort—though the fly ultimately succumbs to flattery, underscoring self-vigilance.**[^1][^2]

In both, the powerful entity initiates with an invitation or taunt that belittles the other's stature while masking self-interest:

- The spider flatters the fly about her beauty and his "humble hut" with "fine curtains" and "soft beds," but the fly initially sees through it: "May God protect me from your beds so soft, / For those who sleep there are forever lost." She falls anyway after more praise.[^3]
- The mountain haughtily mocks the squirrel's size—"A tiny thing, with such conceit"—claiming unmatchable "majesty," but the squirrel counters that "In everything, the might of God is manifest," valuing function over bulk: "You cannot take a single step... / ...try to crack this simple nut."[^4]

This structure critiques hubris and deception in the mighty, teaching that ethical discernment lies in recognizing diverse divine purposes ("No single thing in this world is in vain"[^5]), resisting flattery, and affirming self-worth (*khudi*) beyond appearances—core to Iqbal's philosophy.[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 20 "Ek Makra aur Makhi" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/)).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 21 "Ek Pahar aur Gilehri" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/)).

[^3]: Ibid., ch. 20 ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-02078b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-02078b)), ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-b6f251](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-b6f251](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-b6f251)), ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-5487cd](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/20-ek-makra-aur-makhi/translation/#p-5487cd)).

[^4]: Ibid., ch. 21 ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-a2b453](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-a2b453)), ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-b8e6bc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-b8e6bc)), ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-fa7390](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-fa7390)), ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-20c7d7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-20c7d7)).

[^5]: Ibid., ch. 21 ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4ddc6a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/21-ek-pahar-aur-gilehri/translation/#p-4ddc6a)).

[^6]: The corpus lacks explicit Iqbal commentary on these fables here, but their shared animal-dialogue form echoes classical fable ethics (e.g., via *Kalila wa Dimna* influences on Iqbal), emphasizing functional equality in creation.
